coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence “Turn OFF-ON” detection of Fe3+ and propiconazole pesticide using blue emissive carbon dots from lemon peel
coredata.dc:creator: Vadia, Foziya Yusuf
Ghosh, Subhadeep
Mehta, Vaibhavkumar N.
Jha, Sanjay
Malek, Naved I.
Park, Tae Jung
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this study, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) were employed as a novel fluorescence “turn OFF-ON” sensor to detect Fe3+ ions in pharmaceutical sample and propiconazole (PC) in food samples. Blue fluorescent “LPCDs” are synthesized from the lemon peel that exhibited emission at 468 nm when excited at 378 nm. The average size of the as-prepared LPCDs is 2.03 nm, displaying a quantum yield of 32 %. Fluorescence “turn OFF-ON” strategy was developed for sensing of Fe3+ ion and PC, demonstrating favorable linearity in the range of 0.5–180 μM and 0.1–40 μM with the detection limits of 0.18 μM and 0.054 μM for Fe3+ and PC, respectively. Further, LPCDs-loaded cellulose paper was used as visual reader to detect Fe3+ and PC. This approach was effectively applied to detect Fe3+ and PC in pharmaceutical and vegetable samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+
Propiconazole
Fluorescence turn OFF-ON mechanism
Analytical method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: LPCDs cellulose paper for detection of Fe3+ and propiconazole
Estimation of Fe3+ and propiconazole in real samples
Fluorescence “Turn OFF-ON” assay of Fe3+ and propiconazole
LPCDs-coated paper strips for the detection of Fe3+ ion and PC
Analysis of Fe3+ and propiconazole in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence excitation (λEx: 378 nm) and emission (λEm: 468 nm) spectra of LPCDs and UV–visible absorption spectrum of LPCDs. Inset: Image of LPCDs in daylight and under UV lamp at 365 nm.
TEM images at 10 nm scale bars of (a) LPCDs, (b) LPCDs + Fe3+, (c) LPCDs-Fe3++PC. Histograms are plotted for (d) LPCDs, (e) LPCDs + Fe3+, (c) LPCDs-Fe3++PC at 10 nm scale bar.

(a) Emission spectra of LPCDs at 468 nm with addition of different metal ions (Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ni2+, 1.0 mM) solutions. Inset: Image of LPCDs solutions with the addition of above metal ions under 365 nm of UV light. (b) Emission spectra of LPCDs-Fe3+ system λEm at 468 nm with the addition of various pesticides (cupper oxychloride, isoprothiolane, hexaconazole, tricyclazole, tebuconazole, carbendazim, and PC; 1.0 mM). Inset: Image of LPCDs-Fe3+ solutions with the addition of above pesticides under 365 nm of UV light.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of LPCDs with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0.5–180 µM). Inset: Photographic image of LPCDs solutions with different concentration of Fe3+ ion (0.5–180 µM). (b) Fluorescence spectra of LPCDs-Fe3+ system in the presence of increasing concentration of PC (0.1–40 µM). Inset: Photographic image of LPCDs-Fe3+ ion with various amounts of PC under 365 nm of UV light.

LPCDs coated cellulose paper strips for (a) the detection of Fe3+ (2, 10, 40, 90 and 180 µM) and (b) the fluorescence recovery of LPCDs with PC (0.1, 0.8, 5, 20 and 40 µM). The photographic images were taken under 365 nm of UV light.
Comparison of the present method with the other reported fluorescence methods for the detection of Fe3+ ion.
Comparison of the present method with the conventional analytical methods for the detection of PC.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Time-resolved fluorescence microspheres lateral flow immunochromatographic assay.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence “Turn OFF-ON” detection of Fe3+ and propiconazole pesticide using blue emissive carbon dots from lemon peel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ASPEE SHAKILAM Biotechnology Institute, Navsari Agricultural University, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ASPEE SHAKILAM Biotechnology Institute, Navsari Agricultural University, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) were employed as a novel fluorescence “turn OFF-ON” sensor to detect Fe3+ ions in pharmaceutical sample and propiconazole (PC) in food samples. Blue fluorescent “LPCDs” are synthesized from the lemon peel that exhibited emission at 468 nm when excited at 378 nm. The average size of the as-prepared LPCDs is 2.03 nm, displaying a quantum yield of 32 %. Fluorescence “turn OFF-ON” strategy was developed for sensing of Fe3+ ion and PC, demonstrating favorable linearity in the range of 0.5–180 μM and 0.1–40 μM with the detection limits of 0.18 μM and 0.054 μM for Fe3+ and PC, respectively. Further, LPCDs-loaded cellulose paper was used as visual reader to detect Fe3+ and PC. This approach was effectively applied to detect Fe3+ and PC in pharmaceutical and vegetable samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that ferric ion (Fe3+) plays key roles in both the environment and the human body. Many essential biological processes, such as cell respiration, oxygen transport, nitrogen fixation, DNA synthesis, electron transfer, and hemoglobin synthesis, depend on it’s chemical reactions since it has special redox and coordination capabilities (Kailasa et al., 2019). It also serves as a cofactor or essential structural component in the above biological processes. Apart from these biological importance, some disorders, including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's syndrome, anemia, and cancer, could develop as a result of extreme or less iron in the body. Thus, accurate measurement of Fe3+ ion concentration was carried out in biological specimens, and it should be within an ideal physiological range, thereby controlling all biochemical pathways of Fe3+ ions in biological processes (Kaplan & Kaplan, 2009). Hemoglobin, a substance in the human body, contains the Fe3+ ion and is crucial for oxygen transport in the bloodstream. In view this, there has been a variety of spectroscopic methods reported for the assessment of Fe3+ ion, including atomic absorption spectrometry (Ghaedi et al., 2008), fluorescence (Raveendran, Babu & Renuka, 2019), UV–visible (Li et al., 2016), inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometric (Pomazal et al., 1999), and mass spectrometric (Spolaor et al., 2012) methods. However, each technique has its own advances and drawbacks, such as highly specialized equipment with complex procedures, some of the techniques require specialized technician and they and are expensive. Thus, the development of green fluorescent sensors with exceptional selectivity for the Fe3+ ion over other trace elements is highly required in order to enable the precise recognition of Fe3+ ion in complex samples.

A fungicide is a particular kind of pesticide that selectively prevents or kills the fungus, thereby protecting the crop yields. Plant pathogens can cause a variety of diseases, which are significantly damaged crop yields (Khan, Gulab & Haider, 2018). Hence, controlling of various diseases in plants is a key element in improving the yields of various crops. It was observed that fungicides are extensively used in agriculture sectors to control fungal diseases during the development of the crop, yielding to protect the loss of the crops, which can also improve the product of crops. Among various fungicides, azole group-based fungicides were frequently used to kill fungi including blights, molds, rusts, and, mildews (Hutson & Miyamoto, 1998). Propiconazole (PC) is one of the fungicide that contains triazole group, and widely used to treat various fungal diseases in grains, fruits, and vegetables. Moreover, PC is a class of conazole fungicide with 1,2,4-triazole functional group (Chen, Moore & Nesnow, 2008). It is a systemic fungicide that defends against leaf spots, powdery mildew, and, rust disease. It acts by interfering with ergosterol biosynthesis and inhibiting steroid demethylation. However, there are several concerns about exposure of PC to the environment, which can adversely affect to insects, zooplankton, mammals, fish, etc. (Zhang et al., 2015). As a result, monitoring of PC in food and environmental samples plays a key role in assessing the food and vegetables quality and safety. Numerous analytical studies including liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) method, high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with UV detector (HPLC-UV) (Zrostlıkova et al., 2002), gas chromatography coupled with electron capture detector (GC-ECD), GC with nitrogen/phosphorus detector (GC-NPD) (Šťávová et al., 2011), GC with flame ionization detector (GC-FID) (Rasmussen et al., 2011) and solid phase desorption ion mobility spectrometry (SPD - IMS) (Umapathi et al., 2021) have been used to estimate the concentration of PC in environmental and food samples. Although these studies have successfully demonstrated for the quantification of PC in real samples but they exhibit several demerits such as requirement of high sample/solvent volume, multiple extractions with long periods of time, the need for a skilled technician, time-consuming and non-portable (Zheng et al., 2015). To overcome above mentioned demerits, the fabrication of an eco-friendly fluorescent probe is highly needed to detect both Fe3+ and PC in real samples with miniaturized sample preparation protocols.

A novel class of promising nanomaterials with photoluminescent properties known as carbon dots (CDs) has gained much attention especially in optical sensing applications. These are carbon nanostructures having the size < 10 nm and successfully utilized in chemical sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, electrocatalysis, and optoelectronics (Mehta et al., 2014; Yusuf et al., 2022). Noticeably, CDs exhibit extensive merits such as low toxicity, simple preparation, high water solubility, high quantum yield, good biocompatibility, and unique optical properties (Yusuf et al., 2022). Recently, CDs-based fluorescence methods have been used to detect pesticides, drugs and biomolecules (Zou et al., 2017; Li et al., 2016; Yusuf et al., 2022) and Fe3+ (Bhamore et al., 2017; Miao et al., 2015) with remarkable analytical features in comparison to other analytical methods.

Herein, we explore the analytical potential of blue emissive LPCDs for the detection of Fe3+ and PC via fluorescence “turn OFF-ON” mechanism. The blue fluorescent LPCDs were effectively prepared from lemon peel (as carbon source) using phosphoric acid as a strong oxidizing agent. Importantly, lemon peel contains a rich composition of fiber, pectin, flavonoids, and essential oil constituents, thereby allowing to fabricate blue emissive LPCDs with unique active functional groups (COR, OH, CHO and COOH and NH2) on the surfaces of LPCDs The as-fabricated LPCDs are highly stable and well dispersed in water, displaying λEm/Ex at 468/378 nm. The fluorescence “turn OFF-ON” sensor exhibited good linearities in the concentrations ranges of 0.5–180 and of 0.1–40 μM with the limits of detection 0.18 and 0.054 μM for Fe3+ ion and PC, respectively. Moreover, selectivity study confirms that the LPCDs-based fluorescent probe is non-selective for other metal ions and pesticides. Additionally, LPCDs are coated onto the cellulose paper to develop fluorescence paper strip for sensing of both analytes. Lastly, the as-synthesized LPCDs were effectively used for the detection of Fe3+ in pharmaceutical sample and PC in vegetables.

Lemons were bought from Local Market in Surat, Gujarat. Hexaconazole, and isoprothiolane were collected from Crop Life Sci. Ltd., India. Propiconazole, imidacloprid, tricyclazole, tebuconazole and copper oxychloride were obtained from Atul Limited, Gujarat, India. Carbendazim (Gujarat Insecticides Limited), imazethapyr, kresoxim methyl, and lambda cyhalothrin (Cheminova India Limited) were used in this study. Tonofolic-Z iron tablet (Unison, pharmaceuticals, Pvt. Ltd., Gujarat) was purchased from medical store, Surat, Gujarat, India. NiSO4⋅6H2O, Cr(NO3) ⋅6H2O, Zn(NO3) ⋅6H2O, KBr, Na2SO4, Mn(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, AlCl3, FeSO4, Co(NO3)2 ⋅6H2O, ZnSO4, Cu(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2 FeCl3, K2Cr2O7, ethanol, pyrophosphate, ascorbic acid, sodium citrate, and H3PO4 were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

The synthesized LPCDs from lemon peel were characterized through various analytical instruments. Fluorescence spectra of LPCDs were recorded through Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies, USA., The Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer from Ocean Optics was utilized to measure absorption characteristics of LPCDs. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) is used to know the surface functional groups of LP-CDs (FT-IR APHA II, Bruker, Germany). The size of LPCDs were determined using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with a JEM 2100 instrument from JEOL, Japan. The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of LPCDs were measured by dynamic light scattering spectrometry (DLS) (Horiba, SZ-100).

Lemons (100 g) were cleaned with water and peeled it. The peel was cut into small pieces and stored at −15 ˚C in the freezer. To synthesize LPCDs, 5.0 gm of freeze-cut lemon peel was treated with H3PO4 (40 N) with 10 mL of distilled water. The above solution was sonicated for 20 min and then headed at 160 ˚C for 45 min. The obtained dark brown crude solution was filtered and neutralized with NaOH solution (1.0 M). The obtained LPCDs were stored at 5 ˚C for further studies. A schematic illustration of LPCDs synthesis and its application is shown in Scheme S1 of Supporting Information.

In the experiment, fluorescence detection of Fe3+ was accomplished as follows: stock solution (1.0 mM) of nine metal ions (Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ni2+) were prepared from their respective salts. Then, 0.5 mL of above-mentioned metal ions solution was taken in 4 mL vial separately, and added 1.0 mL of LPCDs to each vial of metal ions solution. Vortexed each vial for 120 s and kept aside for 1 min before measuring the fluorescence spectra for the above solutions. The emission spectra of the above samples were recorded upon the excitation at 378 nm.

For the detection of PC, Fe3+ was added to the LPCDs sensing system, which is represented as LPCDs-Fe3+. To this, 1 mM stock solution of PC was prepared by using water and ethanol (1:1, v/v). Various concentrations of PC were prepared and added (0.5 mL) separately to 1.0 mL of LPCDs-Fe3+ system. The sample vials were vortexed for 180 s. The change in the intensity of fluorescence was investigated by measuring the emission spectra at 468 nm when applied λEx at 378 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LPCDs cellulose paper for detection of Fe3+ and propiconazole
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained cellulose Whatman filter paper was cut into a 1 cm diameter circle. The circular paper was then soaked in 5 mL of LPCDs solution for 10 min. After soaking, the paper was left to dry for 30 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence color change of LPCDs loaded cellulose paper was examined after exposure to Fe3+ ion concentrations (2, 10, 40, 90 and 180 µM). The cellulose paper was removed from the solution and allowed to dry for 30 min. The changes in the fluorescence of paper strips were investigated under UV light at 365 nm. For recovery, the LPCDs-Fe3+ treated paper strips were immersed in various concentrations of PC (0.1,0.8, 5, 20 and 40 µM) and the fluorescence recovery in LPCDs was observed by exposing the papers to a UV lamp with a 365 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of Fe3+ and propiconazole in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared LPCDs were used for the detection of Fe3+ in Tonofolic -Z iron tablets. Briefly, 5 mg of Tonofolic -Z iron tablet was crushed and then removed unwanted red coating material from the crushed tablet. After that, different stock solutions (2.5, 1.25, and 0.625 mg/mL) of the tablet were prepared by dissolving it in 2 mL of aqua regia solution (1 HNO3:3 HCl). The above solution was heated to get solid product and added 5 mL of water, and then dilute it up to 100 mL. The concentration of Fe3+ in the Tonofolic -Z tablet was calculated from the calibration graph via the standard addition method. For propiconazole, the developed LPCDs-Fe3+ system was used to estimate PC in vegetables (corn and mushroom). The above vegetable samples were purchased from a local market and utilized without any additional treatment. Corn and mushroom were washed and chopped with a stainless-steel knife. Take 2.0 g of corn sample, and spiked different concentrations of PC (25, 30, 35 and 40 μM) and kept aside for 24 h in dark. Furthermore, add 5 mL of methanol and 5 mL of water to the spiked sample, and then sonicated for 10 min. The spiked solution is filtered with Whatman filter paper (Grade- 292) and the concentration of PC was estimated by the described procedure earlier. For mushroom samples, 2.0 g of mushroom paste was spiked with the above mentioned different concentrations of PC. The spiked samples were put in the dark for 24 h. Then, added ethanol:water (1:1. v/v) and then sonicated for 15 min and the amount of PC was estimated by the described procedure earlier.

In this study, lemon peel was used as a natural carbon source for the synthesis of LPCDs through the acid carbonization method. H3PO4 was used as oxidizing and doping agent, which offers to improve fluorescence properties of CDs via molecular fluorescence mechanism (Gong et al., 2015). To examine the optical characteristics of the as-synthesized LPCDs, UV visible and fluorescence spectra were measured. The as-synthesized LPCDs show blue emission color under UV lamp (365 nm) and yellowish in daylight (Inset of Fig. 1 ). The optimum emission peak of LPCDs was measured by examining emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (300–400 nm). The optimum emission peak was perceived at 468 nm when excited at 378 nm (Fig. S1a-b of Supporting Information). With an increase in excitation wavelength, the emission peak was likewise moved to a higher wavelength, which is explained by the “edge red-shift effect” (Yan et al., 2016). Fig. 1 illustrates the excitation, emission spectra, and UV– visible spectrum of LPCDs. The stability of the LPCDs was monitored for a period of 70 days, revealing that the LPCDs exhibited remarkable stability over time, although they tended to aggregate at intervals, resulting in a decrease in their intensity (Fig. S2 of Supporting Information).

To examine the surface functionality of the synthesized LPCDs, the FT-IR study was achieved (Fig. S3 of Supporting Information). The peaks were observed around 3200–3500 cm−1 belonged to OH and NH stretching. A sharp peak of CC stretching was noticed around 1656 cm−1 and the average peak at 2428 cm−1 for PH stretching. The distinctive peaks at 1364 and 1082 cm−1 are attributed to PO stretching and PH2 bending. A distinctive peak at 858 is assigned to CC bending vibrations in LPCDs (Dong et al., 2020). Moreover, the absorbance peak at 325 nm was noticed in the UV region which suggested the presence of electronic absorption transitions of π–π* and n–π*, confirming the existence of CO, CC, and CO, surface functionality on the LPCDs (Fig. S4 of Supporting Information). The quantum yield of LPCDs blue emission was calculated using a standard equation and found to be 32%. The shape and particle size distribution of the LPCDs were investigated using HR-TEM (Fig. 2 ). It was noticed that the LPCDs has an average diameter of 2.03 ± 0.74 nm, which confirmed that a reasonably narrow particle size distribution between 1.0 and 3.0 nm, and aggregation has not been observed, as shown in Fig. 2a. The histogram was plotted for the calculation of particle size distribution of LPCDs, LPCDs + Fe3+, and LPCDs - Fe3+ + PC (Fig. 2d-f). The HR-TEM measurements are supported by the DLS, which demonstrate that the average hydrodynamic diameter of LPCDs is 4.4 nm (Fig. S5a of Supporting Information). It can be observed that the diameter of LPCDs measured by DLS is slightly larger (∼2.4 nm) as compared to HR-TEM image, which is due to the measurement of hydrated molecules on the surface of LPCDs. Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to explore the surface elements of as-prepared LPCDs. A survey spectrum display the peaks at 131.87, 285.35, 399.78, 530.83, and 1072.36 eV which can be attributed to P2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Na1s respectively shown in Fig. S6a of Supporting Information. The C1s spectrum showed two peaks at 285.2 and 287.7 eV (Fig. S6b of Supporting Information) which belongs to CC, and CO, respectively. Fig. S6c of Supporting Information shows the N1s spectrum, which demonstrated distinct peak at approximately 399.7 eV. This peak can be attributed to the NH group. The amplified peak is depicted in Fig. S6d of Supporting Information to accurately depict the peak of oxygen. Two fitted peaks at 531.7 and 534.8 eV correspond to the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively (Atulbhai, Swapna & Kailasa, 2023). Moreover, a peak at 1072 eV is attributed to Na1s that might originate from NaOH. Another peak at 131.8 eV corresponds to P2p, which confirms the doping of phosphorus into CDs. The contents of the elements in the LPCDs were 19.43% (C), 49.78% (O), 1.21% (N), 13.98% (P), and 15.6% (Na), respectively. The spectroscopic analysis successfully demonstrated the formation of fluorescent LPCDs with ultra-small size and different active surface functionality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “Turn OFF-ON” assay of Fe3+ and propiconazole
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate sensing applications of LPCDs toward metal ions, the as-synthesized LPCDs were examined with different metal ions (Cu2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ni2+, 1.0 mM) and the measured spectra were shown in Fig. 3 a. It was noticed that Fe3+ shows an obvious change in the emission intensity of LPCDs, signifying that the LPCDs could be used as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ assay via turn-off mechanism. To recover the fluorescence of LPCDs, 0.5 mL of various pesticides solutions (copper oxychloride, isoprothiolane, hexaconazole, tricyclazole, tebuconazole, carbendazim and PC, 1.0 mM) were added into the LPCDs-Fe3+ system and the spectral changes were investigated (Fig. 3b). Noticeably, the emission spectrum of LPCDs was remarkably recovered (∼90%) with the addition of PC, offering to establish analytical strategy for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ ion and PC via fluorescence turn OFF-ON mechanism. The response time for the detection of Fe3+ was also examined at different time intervals of 1–5 min, displaying that the maximum quenching was observed at 5 min with the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. S7a of Supporting Information). Moreover, the response time for restoring of fluorescence of LPCDs in the presence of PC was also investigated at different time intervals (1–15 min) (Fig. S7b of Supporting Information), revealing the fluorescence was effectively restored within 10 min, which signifies that LPCDs acted as a rapid fluorescence turn OFF-ON sensor for parallel detection of Fe3+ ion and PC.

The influence of pH was investigated on the fluorescence turn OFF-ON assay of Fe3+ ion and PC. To this, the emission spectra of LPCDs were analysed without and with the addition of Fe3+ and PC in acetate buffer pH (2.0–12.0) (Fig. S8 in the Supporting Information). The emission spectral results revealed that in the absence of Fe3+ and PC, the emission spectra of the LPCDs remained almost constant at acetate pH 2.0–12.0. Noticeably, the fluorescence quenching and restoring of LPCDs were noticed at acetate buffer pH 7.0 (Fig. S9 of Supporting Information), suggesting pH 7.0 is the optimum pH for fluorescence turn OFF-ON assays of Fe3+ ion and PC.

To investigate the fluorescence turn OFF-ON strategy for Fe3+ ion and PC assay using LPCDs as a probe, HR-TEM, DLS, zeta potential and life time analyses were performed. Initially, the dispersion and aggregation of LPCDs in the presence of both analytes (Fe3+ ion and PC) were verified using HR-TEM analysis. The size of the LPCDs (2.03 ± 0.74 nm) is considerably increased to 8.12 ± 1.08 nm when Fe3+ is added to the LPCDs solution, as shown in Fig. 2c. Remarkably, the LPCDs + Fe3+ aggregates are dramatically altered the size of LPCDs (4.35 ± 0.43 nm) with the addition of PC (Fig. 2d), revealing the size of LPCDs-Fe3+ ion aggregates is noticeably changed upon the addition of PC. Also, the DLS measurements of LPCDs + Fe3+ (14.7 nm) and LPCDs-Fe3++PC (7.7 nm) display a significant change in the average hydrodynamic diameter of LPCDs (4.4 nm), which is consistent with the TEM data as shown in Fig. S5b-cof Supporting Information. To know the possible quenching mechanism of the as-synthesized LPCDs with Fe3+, the XPS analysis revealed that the surface of LPCDs is rich with oxygen in comparison to other elements. The OH groups on LPCDs could react with Fe3+, favoring to form LPCDs-Fe3+ ion complex, which results the aggregation of LPCDs (Atulbhai, Swapna & Kailasa, 2023). Similarly, LPCDs-Fe3+ ion aggregates are effectively segregated upon the introduction of PC (Fig. 2 and Fig. S5b-cof Supporting Information). Since PC has a triazole ring in the structure that consists of a number of donor atoms (nitrogen and oxygen), favoring to form a coordination complex between PC and Fe3+ ion, which results to detachment of Fe3+ ion from LPCDs surface. As a result, the quenched fluorescence of LPCDs was remarkably restored, which suggests that LPCDs act as a probe for the detection of Fe3+ ion and PC. Additionally, the surface charge of the as-prepared LPCDs was measured by zeta potential (Fig. S10a-c of Supporting Information). It was observed that the LPCDs carried negative charge of −36.6 mV, whereas it decreased to −15.8 mV with Fe3+ ion, however, again the negative charge is increased to −28.1 mV with the addition of PC. These results illustrated that the occurrence of a strong electrostatic interaction between the LPCDs and Fe3+, resulting to form LPCDs-Fe3+ ion aggregates. Interestingly, LPCDs-Fe3+ ion aggregates were segregated by the addition of PC due to the formation of PC-Fe3+ ion complex, which favors to detach Fe3+ ion from LPCDs surface. Further, the lifetime analysis was conducted to determine the decay time of LPCDs in the presence of both analytes separately (Fig. S11 of Supporting Information). The fluorescence lifetime of as-synthesized LPCDs was 1.35 ns, however, the decay time of LPCDs + Fe3+ and LPCDs-Fe3++PC was 1.28 ns and 1.29 ns, respectively. The results suggested that the lifetime of LPCDs was unchanged with the addition of Fe3+ and PC which reveals the static quenching due to the formation of non-fluorescent complex at the ground state (Sahoo et al., 2020). The FT-IR data revealed that the intensities of OH and NH peaks were changed when LPCDs interacted with the Fe3+ (Fig. S3 of Supporting Information). In the case of propiconazole, the intensities of characteristic peaks were noticeably decreased due to the formation of PC-Fe3+ ion complex. To further support the sensing mechanism, the absorption spectra of LPCDs, LPCDs + Fe3+, and LPCDs-Fe3+ + PC were studied (Fig. S4 of Supporting Information). The as-prepared LPCDs showed an absorption peak at 325 nm, but when Fe3+ was added the peak significantly decreased and half of the absorption spectrum is overlapped with excitation spectrum of LPCDs, which might be due to inner filter effect (Yan et al., 2016). Hence, the intensity of LPCDs was quenched with addition of Fe3+ ion and the quenched fluorescence intensity of LPCDs is restored by the addition of PC, demonstrating the fluorescence “OFF-ON” mechanism for Fe3+ and PC assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LPCDs-coated paper strips for the detection of Fe3+ ion and PC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to extend the sensing applications of LPCDs in assaying of Fe3+ ion and PC in real sample, cellulose paper was cut into multiple circles with 1 cm diameter, immersed in the LPCDs solution for 10 min, and allowed to dry at room temperature. Next, the dried papers were soaked with various concentrations of Fe3+ solutions (2, 10, 40, 90 and 180 µM) and subsequently left to dry at ambient temperature for 30 min. The blue emission of LPCDs was monitored under 365 nm of UV light, exhibiting a noticeable change in fluorescence color (dark to light blue), in response to increasing concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. 5 a). Similarly, LPCDs-Fe3+ ion-paper strip was treated with different concentration of PC (0.1, 0.8, 5, 20 and 40 µM) and analyzed the fluorescence changes under 365 nm of UV light (Fig. 5b), displaying a significant appearance of blue fluorescence with increasing concentration of PC. These results demonstrated that LPCDs could be used as a probe for the development of miniaturized analytical device for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ ion and PC, respectively.

To investigate the selectivity of LPCDs for the detection of Fe3+ and PC, the impact of various chemical interfering species was examined, including a mixture of cations (Co2+, Mn2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+), anions (Cl-, Br-, SO4 2- andNO3 -), pesticides (imidacloprid, imazethapyr, kresoxim-methyl and lambda-cyhalothrin) was examined on the fluorescence intensity of LPCDs (Fig. S13a of Supporting Information). These spectral results indicated that the addition of the aforesaid chemical species did not show any obvious effect on the emission intensities of LPCDs. Prominently, the fluorescence intensity of LPCDs was only quenched by the addition of Fe3+ ion (Fig. S13b of Supporting Information) and restored the fluorescence of LPCDs with the addition of PC into LPCDs-Fe3+ system, signifying the LPCDs exhibited good selectivity toward Fe3+ ion and PC, respectively (Fig. S13c of Supporting Information). Moreover, the selectivity of LPCDs was also performed using other metal ions (Fe2+, Al3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+, 1.0 mM) with Fe3+ and Fe3+ + PC solution and the spectral data were shown in Fig. S14a-b of Supporting Information. These spectral data confirmed that only Fe3+ ions quenched the maximum fluorescence intensity of LPCDs and the optimum recovery was noticed only after the addition of PC to LPCDs-Fe3+ system. Further, some interfering chemicals such as pyrophosphate, ascorbic acid, and sodium citrate have shown strong affinity toward Fe3+ ions. The interfering study for the recovery of fluorescence intensity was also examined in the presence of these chemicals, revealing that the above interfering species did not interfere during the detection of PC (Fig. S15 of Supporting Information), which confirms that the LPCDs-Fe3+ system is highly selective toward only PC. Overall, the emission spectral data revealed that the existence of the above-mentioned chemical species did not significantly vary the emission spectra of LPCDs, while the addition of Fe3+ causes significant fluorescence quenching of LPCDs, whereas the quenched fluorescence was restored only with the addition of PC. These results confirmed that LPCDs could be used as fluorescence turn “OFF-ON” probe for the simultaneous recognition of Fe3+ and PC, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ and propiconazole in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In the wake of investigating the sensitivity and selectivity of LPCDs with regard to Fe3+ and PC, the practical application of LPCDs was evaluated for the quantification of Fe3+ and PC in real samples. For this, iron tablet (Tonofolic-Z) solution was prepared as per the described procedure in experimental section and prepared different concentrations (2.5, 1.25, and 0.625 mg/mL). The concentration of Fe3+ was estimated by using LPCDs as a sensor. This method exhibited good recovery in the range of 97.0–100.0% with relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 0.10 to 0.2% for the analysis of Fe3+ ion in pharmaceutical sample (Table S1 of Supporting Information). In the case of PC, the collected vegetables (corn and mushroom) were spiked separately with different concentrations of PC (25, 30, 35 and 40 μM). The amount of PC was estimated by the described procedure, displaying good recovery values from 98.0 to 100.0% with RSD ranging from 0.05 to 0.14% for the vegetable samples (Table S2 of Supporting Information). These results demonstrated that LPCDs could be used as promising miniaturized analytical device for parallel detection of Fe3+ ion in pharmaceutical sample and PC in vegetables, respectively with good reliability and accuracy.

In summary, a sustainable fluorescent sensor was developed for the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and PC using LPCDs as a probe via fluorescence turn OFF-ON mechanism. The LPCDs were fabricated by acid carbonization method using H3PO4 as oxidizing and doping agent. The as-fabricated LPCDs displayed blue emission at 468 nm upon excitation at 378 nm. The present method demonstrates good linearities in the concentration ranges of 0.5–40 and of 0.1–40 µM with the LODs of 0.18 and 0.054 μM for Fe3+ and PC, respectively. Furthermore, the cellulose-based paper sensor was developed by coating LPCDs on paper and utilized as a sensor for the visual detection of Fe3+ and PC, respectively. The practical utility of the probe was evaluated for the analysis of Fe3+ in pharmaceutical samples and PC in vegetables, exhibiting with good recoveries with lower RSD values. These results suggested that the as-prepared LPCDs could be explored as suitable candidates for the development of a simple analytical platform to simultaneous assay of Fe3+ in iron tablets and PC in vegetables.

Foziya Yusuf Vadia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Subhadeep Ghosh: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Vaibhavkumar N. Mehta: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Sanjay Jha: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Naved I. Malek: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tae Jung Park: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Suresh Kumar Kailasa: Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under Women Scientist Scheme-A (DST/WOS-A/CS-77/2021). FYV, NIM and SKK sincerely acknowledge the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India, for providing the necessary infrastructure to carry out this work. This work was financially supported by Gujarat State Biotechnology Mission (GSBTM), DST, Govt. of Gujarat, India (BR-04 (GSBTM/JD(R&D)662/2022-23/00292169).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136796.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zou, S., Hou, C., Fa, H., Zhang, L., Ma, Y., Dong, L.,Li, D.,Huo, D., & Yang, M. (2017). An efficient fluorescent probe for fluazinam using N, S co-doped carbon dots from L-cysteine. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 239, 1033-1041. https://doi.org/10.1016/J.SNB.2016.07.169.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zrostlıkova et al., 2002

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alternative calibration approaches to compensate the effect of co-extracted matrix components in liquid chromatography-electrospray ionisation tandem mass spectrometry analysis of pesticide residues in plant materials
Journal of Chromatography A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zrostlıkova, J., Hajšlová, J., Poustka, J., & Begany, P. (2002). Alternative calibration approaches to compensate the effect of co-extracted matrix components in liquid chromatography-electrospray ionisation tandem mass spectrometry analysis of pesticide residues in plant materials. Journal of Chromatography A, 973(1-2), 13-26. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0021-9673(02)01196-2.
